questions.answer: C. {w | (a+b)* and n_a(w) != 0} and {eps | w = epsilon}
questions.to_find: The Myhill-Nerode equivalence classes for the language generated by the given regular grammar

understand.plain: The grammar with production rules S -> aS | a starting from S generates exactly the language a^+ (one or more a's). The Myhill-Nerode theorem partitions all strings over the alphabet into equivalence classes based on how they behave as prefixes — two strings x and y are equivalent if for every string z, xz is in the language iff yz is in the language.

keywords.explain: A partition of Sigma* where two strings x, y are equivalent (x ~_L y) if for all z in Sigma*, xz in L iff yz in L. The number of classes equals the minimum DFA state count.
keywords.example: For L = a*, epsilon ~ a ~ aa because appending any string z gives the same membership result.

keywords.explain: The Myhill-Nerode relation is right-invariant: if x ~ y then xz ~ yz for all z. This means the future behavior (what can be appended to stay in the language) is the same.
keywords.example: For L = {a}, only {a} is accepted, so epsilon and aa are in the same dead class.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='170' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='95' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Class 1: epsilon</text><text x='95' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(not started yet)</text><rect x='220' y='10' width='170' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='305' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Class 2: a, aa, aaa...</text><text x='305' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(n_a >= 1, accepted)</text><text x='200' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L = a^+: two Myhill-Nerode classes</text><text x='200' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Class 1 (dead: epsilon cannot reach L)</text><text x='200' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Class 2 (live: strings with at least one a)</text><line x1='95' y1='70' x2='95' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='305' y1='70' x2='305' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two equivalence classes for L = a^+: Class 1 is epsilon (dead state, cannot lead to acceptance by appending anything useful), Class 2 is all strings with at least one 'a' (can be the full word itself or extended).

given.aim: Find the Myhill-Nerode equivalence classes for the language L = a^+ generated by the grammar S -> aS | a.

terms.meaning: Generates exactly the regular language L = {a, aa, aaa, ...} = a^+
terms.example: S => aS => aaS => aaa (three steps to produce 'aaa')
terms.connects: Defines the target language whose Myhill-Nerode classes we need to find.

terms.meaning: Set of all strings y such that for all z, xz in L iff yz in L
terms.example: For L=a^+, [a] = {a, aa, ...} since appending any string gives the same outcome.
terms.connects: Determines the minimum number of DFA states and partitions inputs.

terms.meaning: The class of strings from which the language cannot be reached by any continuation
terms.example: For L = a^+, epsilon is a dead state: no z makes epsilon*z = z be in a^+ unless z itself is in a^+, but we compare to a itself.
terms.connects: Epsilon forms one class; all strings with at least one a form another class.

given.plan: Determine L from the grammar, then apply the Myhill-Nerode theorem by finding the right-congruence classes. For L = a^+, check which strings have the same future: epsilon (nothing appended keeps it in language) vs non-epsilon strings with at least one a (they are already potentially in L).

steps.title: Determine the language generated by the grammar
steps.apply: Starting from S, every derivation must eventually apply S -> a as the base case. So S generates: a, aa, aaa, ... = a^+. The language L = {a^n | n >= 1}.
steps.note: The grammar only has terminal 'a', so the language is purely over {a}.

steps.title: Identify Myhill-Nerode equivalence classes
steps.formula_raw: x ~_L y iff forall z: xz in L <=> yz in L
steps.note: The minimum DFA for a^+ has exactly 2 states: start (non-accepting) and accept (after reading at least one a), matching the 2 equivalence classes.

solution.result: The two Myhill-Nerode equivalence classes are: {epsilon} (or strings not yet having any a) and {w in a^+ | n_a(w) >= 1}. This corresponds to option C: one class for strings with at least one a, and one class for epsilon.

questions.formula_note: Myhill-Nerode theorem: the number of equivalence classes of the right-invariant relation ~_L equals the number of states in the minimal DFA for L.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE:
Regularity is preserved under the operation of string reversal.

A. TRUE
B. FALSE
questions.to_find: Whether the reverse of a regular language is also regular

understand.plain: If L is a regular language, is L^R = {w^R | w in L} (all strings reversed) also regular? This is a closure property question. Regular languages are closed under reversal, meaning reversing every string in a regular language produces another regular language.

keywords.explain: If w = a_1 a_2 ... a_n, then w^R = a_n ... a_2 a_1. For a language L, L^R = {w^R | w in L}.
keywords.example: If L = {ab, aab}, then L^R = {ba, baa}.

keywords.explain: A language class is closed under an operation if applying that operation to languages in the class always produces a language in the same class.
keywords.example: Regular languages are closed under union: if L1 and L2 are regular, L1 union L2 is regular.

understand.visual_alt: Regular languages are closed under reversal. If DFA M accepts L, construct NFA for L^R by reversing all transitions and swapping start/accept states, then convert to DFA.

given.aim: Determine whether L^R is regular whenever L is regular.

terms.meaning: A regular language over some alphabet Sigma
terms.connects: The input language whose reversal we examine.

terms.meaning: The reverse of L: {w^R | w in L}
terms.example: If L = (ab)*, then L^R = (ba)* which is also regular.
terms.connects: The language we want to show is regular.

terms.meaning: To show closure, we construct a recognizer for L^R from a recognizer for L.
terms.example: Reverse all transitions of the DFA for L, make old start state the new accept state, add new start state with epsilon-transitions to all old accept states.
terms.connects: Gives an NFA for L^R, which can be converted to a DFA, proving regularity.

given.plan: Prove by construction: given a DFA for L, build an NFA for L^R by reversing all edges, swapping start and accept states. Since every NFA has an equivalent DFA, L^R is regular. Hence the statement is TRUE.

steps.title: Construction of NFA for L^R
steps.formula_raw: delta^R(q, a) defined from delta of original DFA
steps.apply:
Given DFA M = (Q, Sigma, delta, q_0, F) for L:
1. Create NFA M^R = (Q union {q_new}, Sigma, delta^R, q_new, {q_0})
2. For each transition delta(p, a) = q in M, add transition delta^R(q, a) contains p in M^R.
3. q_new is the new start state with epsilon-transitions to all states in F.
This NFA accepts exactly L^R.
steps.note: Reversing transitions and swapping initial/final roles is the standard proof technique.

steps.title: Conclude regularity
steps.apply: The NFA M^R constructed above recognizes L^R. Since every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA (subset construction), L^R is accepted by a DFA. Therefore L^R is regular.
steps.note: This proves that regular languages are closed under reversal, so the statement is TRUE.

solution.result: TRUE — Regularity is preserved under string reversal. If L is regular, then L^R is also regular.

questions.formula_note: Closure of regular languages under reversal: proved by reversing DFA transitions and swapping start/accept roles to get an NFA for L^R, then applying NFA-to-DFA conversion.

questions.question:
State whether the following statement is TRUE or FALSE:
All subsets of regular sets are regular.

A. TRUE
B. FALSE
questions.to_find: Whether every subset of a regular language is also regular

understand.plain: A regular language may have many subsets, and not all of them need to be regular. We only need one counterexample to show the statement is FALSE. For instance, {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is a subset of (a|b)* (which is regular) but is itself not regular.

keywords.explain: L1 is a subset of L2 if every string in L1 is also in L2.
A language that cannot be recognized by any finite automaton; requires a model with unbounded memory like a PDA.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 0} requires counting, which a finite automaton cannot do.

understand.visual_alt: The regular language (a+b)* contains all strings; the non-regular language {a^n b^n | n>=0} is a subset of it, showing not every subset of a regular language is regular.

given.aim: Disprove that all subsets of regular languages are regular by finding a counterexample.

terms.meaning: The set of all strings over {a, b}, which is regular.
terms.example: (a+b)* accepted by a single-state DFA with self-loops on a and b.
terms.connects: The regular language whose subset we examine.

terms.meaning: The context-free (but non-regular) language of equal numbers of a's followed by b's.
terms.example: epsilon, ab, aabb, aaabbb are all in L_sub.
terms.connects: L_sub is a subset of (a+b)* but is NOT regular (by the pumping lemma).

terms.meaning: Every sufficiently long string in a regular language can be 'pumped' (middle part repeated). If a language fails this, it is not regular.
terms.example: For a^n b^n, pumping the middle of a^p b^p gives a string with unequal a's and b's, not in the language.
terms.connects: Proves L_sub is not regular, making it the required counterexample.

given.plan: Show the statement is FALSE by providing a concrete counterexample: L_sub = {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is a subset of the regular language (a+b)*, yet L_sub is not regular.

steps.title: Identify a regular language and its non-regular subset
steps.apply:
L_reg = (a+b)* is regular (accepted by a trivial one-state DFA).
L_sub = {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is a subset of L_reg since every a^n b^n is a string over {a, b}.
steps.note: The containment L_sub subset (a+b)* is obvious.

steps.title: Show L_sub is not regular using the pumping lemma
steps.formula_raw: Pumping Lemma: |xy| <= p, |y| >= 1, xy^i z in L for all i >= 0
steps.apply:
Assume L_sub is regular with pumping length p. Consider w = a^p b^p in L_sub.
By the pumping lemma, w = xyz where |xy| <= p, |y| >= 1.
Since |xy| <= p, y consists only of a's: y = a^k for some k >= 1.
Pumping: xy^2 z = a^(p+k) b^p. Since k >= 1, p+k != p, so xy^2 z not in L_sub.
Contradiction — L_sub is NOT regular.
steps.note: This is the classic pumping lemma argument for {a^n b^n}.

solution.result: FALSE — Not all subsets of regular languages are regular. Counterexample: {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is a non-regular subset of (a+b)*.

questions.formula_note: Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages: used to prove {a^n b^n} is non-regular, serving as the counterexample to disprove the claim.

questions.question:
Let R_1 and R_2 be regular sets defined over the alphabet Sigma. Then:

A. R_1 intersection R_2 is not regular.
B. R_1 union R_2 is regular.
C. Sigma* - R_1 is regular.
D. R_1* is not regular.
questions.to_find: Which of the given statements about regular sets R_1 and R_2 are true

understand.plain: Regular languages are closed under several operations. Union of two regular languages is always regular. Complement of a regular language (Sigma* minus R_1) is always regular. Intersection and Kleene star also preserve regularity (so A and D are false as stated). We need to identify the TRUE statements.

keywords.explain: A class of languages is closed under an operation if applying the operation to any languages in the class gives a result in the same class.
keywords.example: Regular languages are closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, and Kleene star.

keywords.explain: Sigma* - L is the set of all strings NOT in L. For regular L, the complement is obtained by swapping accepting and non-accepting states in the DFA.
keywords.example: If L = a^+, then Sigma* - L = {epsilon} union {strings with a b} which is also regular.

understand.visual_alt: Regular languages form a Boolean algebra: closed under union, intersection, and complement. Also closed under Kleene star and concatenation.

given.aim: Identify which closure properties of regular languages hold from the given options.

terms.meaning: Set of strings in R_1 or R_2 (or both)
terms.example: If R_1 = a*, R_2 = b*, then R_1 union R_2 = a* union b* which is regular.
terms.connects: Option B — regular languages are closed under union (TRUE).

terms.meaning: Complement of R_1: all strings over Sigma not in R_1
terms.example: If R_1 = a^+, then Sigma* - R_1 = {epsilon, b, ab, ...} which is regular.
terms.connects: Option C — regular languages are closed under complement (TRUE).

terms.meaning: Set of strings in both R_1 and R_2
terms.example: a* intersection b* = {epsilon}, which is regular.
terms.connects: Option A says it is NOT regular — this is FALSE since intersection of regular languages is always regular.

given.plan: Check each option against the known closure properties of regular languages: closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, and Kleene star.

steps.title: Check each option for regular language closure
steps.formula_raw: Regular languages closed under: union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star
steps.apply:
A. R_1 intersection R_2 is NOT regular — FALSE. Intersection of two regular languages IS always regular (closed under intersection).
B. R_1 union R_2 is regular — TRUE. Regular languages are closed under union.
C. Sigma* - R_1 is regular — TRUE. Regular languages are closed under complement (swap accept/reject states in DFA).
D. R_1* is NOT regular — FALSE. Kleene star of a regular language is always regular.
steps.note: All standard closure properties (union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star) hold for regular languages.

steps.title: Confirm TRUE statements
steps.apply:
B is TRUE: If DFA M_1 accepts R_1 and DFA M_2 accepts R_2, the product construction gives a DFA for R_1 union R_2.
C is TRUE: If DFA M accepts R_1, swapping accepting and non-accepting states gives a DFA for Sigma* - R_1.
steps.note: Statements B and C are both true; A and D are false.

solution.result: B and C are TRUE. R_1 union R_2 is regular (option B), and Sigma* - R_1 is regular (option C).

questions.formula_note: Closure properties of regular languages: closed under union (product/NFA construction), complement (DFA state swap), intersection (product construction), concatenation, and Kleene star.

questions.question:
Which of the following is the strongest correct statement about a finite language over some finite alphabet?

A. It could be undecidable.
B. It is Turing-machine recognizable.
C. It is a context-sensitive language.
D. It is a regular language.
E. None of the above.
questions.to_find: The strongest correct statement about a finite language over a finite alphabet

understand.plain: A finite language contains only finitely many strings. Since there are finitely many strings, we can list all of them explicitly. A finite automaton (DFA) can recognize any finite language by creating a 'trie'-like structure. Therefore a finite language is always regular, which is the strongest (most restrictive) class in the Chomsky hierarchy that is correct.

keywords.explain: A language that contains a finite number of strings (possibly zero).
keywords.example: L = {a, ab, abc} is finite with 3 strings.

keywords.explain: A containment hierarchy: Regular subset CFL subset CSL subset Decidable subset RE. 'Strongest' means most restricted (smallest class).
keywords.example: Saying L is regular is a stronger statement than saying L is context-free, since every regular language is context-free but not vice versa.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx='200' cy='160' rx='190' ry='35' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='200' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Recursively Enumerable (RE)</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='135' rx='155' ry='30' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='200' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Decidable / Recursive</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='110' rx='120' ry='25' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='200' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Context-Sensitive (CSL)</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='85' rx='85' ry='20' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='200' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Context-Free (CFL)</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='58' rx='55' ry='18' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='63' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Regular (includes Finite)</text><text x='200' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Chomsky Hierarchy</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Chomsky hierarchy shown as nested ellipses: Regular (innermost, containing finite languages) inside CFL inside CSL inside Recursive inside RE (outermost).

given.aim: Find the most restrictive language class that always contains every finite language over a finite alphabet.

terms.meaning: L is finite means |L| < infinity (L has a finite number of strings)
terms.connects: Such languages can always be recognized by a finite automaton.

terms.meaning: Recognized by a DFA/NFA/regular expression; lowest level in the Chomsky hierarchy
terms.example: L = {a, aa, b} is regular — build a DFA that accepts exactly these 3 strings.
terms.connects: Every finite language is regular: enumerate all strings in a finite DFA trie.

terms.meaning: The most specific (lowest in hierarchy) class that correctly contains all finite languages
terms.example: Saying 'regular' is stronger than saying 'context-free' — it gives more information.
terms.connects: Option D (regular) is both correct and the strongest among the options.

given.plan: Argue that any finite language L = {w_1, w_2, ..., w_k} can be recognized by a DFA built as a trie of all k strings. Since a DFA exists, L is regular. Regular is the most specific (strongest) class, so D is the answer.

steps.title: Show every finite language is regular
steps.formula_raw: L = {w_1, w_2, ..., w_k} => L = {w_1} union {w_2} union ... union {w_k}
steps.apply:
Each singleton language {w} is regular: build a DFA that reads w one symbol at a time and accepts after reading all of w.
The union of finitely many regular languages is regular (closure under union).
Therefore every finite language is regular.
steps.note: Alternatively, any finite language has a regular expression: w_1 | w_2 | ... | w_k.

steps.title: Identify the strongest statement
steps.formula_raw: Regular subset CFL subset CSL subset Decidable subset RE
steps.apply:
A: Undecidable — FALSE. Finite languages are trivially decidable (check if input is one of finitely many strings).
B: Turing-machine recognizable — TRUE but weak (every regular language is TM-recognizable).
C: Context-sensitive — TRUE but weaker than D.
D: Regular — TRUE and the strongest (most specific) correct statement.
E: None of the above — FALSE since D is correct.
steps.note: In the Chomsky hierarchy, regular is the smallest class, so saying 'it is regular' is the strongest possible correct claim.

solution.result: D. It is a regular language — this is the strongest correct statement. Every finite language over a finite alphabet is regular.

questions.formula_note: Every finite language is regular: it can be expressed as a finite union of singleton languages (each of which is trivially regular), and regular languages are closed under finite union.
